<!-- Google tag (gtag.js) -->
<script async src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/gtag/js?id=G-7PRVEBE1EF"></script>
<script>
  window.dataLayer = window.dataLayer || [];
  function gtag(){dataLayer.push(arguments);}
  gtag('js', new Date());

  gtag('config', 'G-7PRVEBE1EF');
</script>

# ML-Stats Dictionary {#sec-dictionary}

```{r}
#| include: false

colourize <- function(x, color) {
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    sprintf("\\textcolor{%s}{%s}", color, x)
  } else if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
    sprintf("<span style='color: %s;'>%s</span>", color,
      x)
  } else x
}
```

Machine learning and statistics comprise a **substantial synergy** that is reflected in data science. Thus, it is imperative to construct solid bridges between both disciplines to ensure everything is clear regarding their tremendous amount of jargon and terminology. This **ML-Stats dictionary** (*ML* stands for *Machine Learning*) aims to be one of these bridges, especially within supervised learning and regression analysis contexts.

![Image by [*Gerd Altmann*](https://pixabay.com/users/geralt-9301/) via [*Pixabay*](https://pixabay.com/photos/definition-books-library-bookshelf-4255486/).](img/definition.jpg){width="550"} 

Below, you will find definitions either highlighted in `r colourize("blue", "blue")` if they correspond to `r colourize("statistical", "blue")` terminology or `r colourize("magenta", "magenta")` if the terminology is `r colourize("machine learning-related", "magenta")`. These definitions come from all **definition admonitions** introduced throughout the fifteen main chapters of this textbook. This colour scheme strives to combine all terminology to switch from one field to another easily. With practice and time, you will be able to jump back and forth when using these concepts.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Attention!
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
Noteworthy terms (either `r colourize("statistical", "blue")` or `r colourize("machine learning-related", "magenta")`) will include a particular admonition identifying which terms (again, either `r colourize("statistical", "blue")` or `r colourize("machine learning-related", "magenta")`) are **equivalent**.
::::
:::

## A {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Alternative hypothesis", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")`, denoted by $H_1$ or $H_a$, is the competing statement about the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")`. It describes the kind of departure from the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` that the test is designed to detect. If the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` restricts the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` to $\Theta_0$, the `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")` often restricts it to another part of the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` space:

$$
H_1 \text{: }
\boldsymbol{\theta}
\in
\Theta_1,
\qquad
\Theta_1 \subseteq \Theta.
$$

For **one-sided tests**, $\Theta_1$ specifies a direction, such as values greater than a benchmark or values less than a target. On the other hand, for **two-sided tests**, $\Theta_1$ specifies departures from the null value in either direction, such as values that are either smaller or larger than the benchmark.

Note that the `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")` determines what counts as "*more extreme*" evidence: 

- For a **right-tailed test**, large positive values of the `r colourize("test statistic", "blue")` support the`r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")`. 
- For a **left-tailed test**, large negative values support the `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")`. 
- For a **two-sided test**, values far from the null value in either direction support the `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")`.

### `r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("attribute", "magenta")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Attributes", "magenta")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("attributes", "magenta")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")`, `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

## C {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Critical value", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("critical value", "blue")` is a cutoff from the `r colourize("null distribution", "blue")` used to decide whether the observed `r colourize("test statistic", "blue")` falls in the rejection region. For a test with `r colourize("significance level", "blue")` $\alpha$, the `r colourize("critical value", "blue")` is chosen so that the rejection region has `r colourize("probability", "blue")` $\alpha$ under the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`.

### `r colourize("Conditional probability", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Suppose $A$ and $B$ are two events in the same `r colourize("sample space", "blue")` $\mathcal{S}$, with

$$
\Pr(B) > 0.
$$

The `r colourize("conditional probability", "blue")` of $A$ given $B$ is defined as

$$
\Pr(A \mid B)
=
\frac{\Pr(A \cap B)}{\Pr(B)}.
$${#eq-conditional-probability-app}

The event $B$ is called the **conditioning event**. The expression $\Pr(A \mid B)$ is read as "*the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of $A$ given $B$*." It describes the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of $A$ after we have restricted attention to situations where $B$ has occurred.

### `r colourize("Confidence interval", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("confidence interval", "blue")` is an interval computed from sample data using a procedure designed to capture the unknown `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` a specified proportion of the time across repeated `r colourize("random samples", "blue")`. For example, a 95% `r colourize("confidence interval", "blue")` procedure is one that, under its assumptions, would produce intervals containing the true `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` in about 95% of repeated samples. For a single observed interval, we do not say that there is a 95% `r colourize("probability", "blue")` that the fixed `r colourize("frequentist parameter", "blue")` lies inside the interval. Instead, we say that the interval was produced by a procedure with 95% long-run coverage.

### `r colourize("Continuous random variable", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` with support $\mathcal{Y}$. If $\mathcal{Y}$ is an uncountably infinite set of possible values, then $Y$ is called a `r colourize("continuous random variable", "blue")`. `r colourize("Continuous random variables", "blue")` can have different kinds of support. For example, they may be:

- **completely unbounded**, with possible values from $-\infty$ to $\infty$;
- **positively unbounded**, with possible values from $0$ to $\infty$;
- **negatively unbounded**, with possible values from $-\infty$ to $0$; or
- **bounded**, with possible values between two finite values $a$ and $b$.

### `r colourize("Covariate", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("covariate", "blue")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Covariates", "blue")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("covariates", "blue")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")`, `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Cumulative distribution function", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`. The `r colourize("cumulative distribution function (CDF)", "blue")` of $Y$ is the function $F_Y(y)$ defined as

$$
F_Y(y) = \Pr(Y \leq y).
$$

The `r colourize("CDF", "blue")` gives the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` that the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` $Y$ takes a value less than or equal to a chosen cutoff value $y$. In this sense, it accumulates `r colourize("probability", "blue")` from the left up to $y$.

For a `r colourize("discrete random variable", "blue")` with `r colourize("probability mass function (PMF)", "blue")` $p_Y(y)$ and support $\mathcal{Y}$, the `r colourize("CDF", "blue")` can be written as

$$
F_Y(y)
=
\sum_{u \in \mathcal{Y}: u \leq y}
p_Y(u).
$$

For a `r colourize("continuous random variable", "blue")` with `r colourize("probability density function (PDF)", "blue")` $f_Y(y)$ and support $\mathcal{Y}$, the `r colourize("CDF", "blue")` can be written as

$$
F_Y(y)
=
\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_Y(u)\,du,
$$

with the understanding that the density is zero outside the support of $Y$.

In these expressions, $y$ is the cutoff value at which the `r colourize("CDF", "blue")` is evaluated, while $u$ is a running variable used inside the summation or integral. This distinction keeps the notation clear: we are accumulating all `r colourize("probability", "blue")` attached to values $u$ that are less than or equal to the cutoff $y$.

In plain words, the `r colourize("PMF", "blue")` or `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` describes how `r colourize("probability", "blue")` is assigned locally, while the `r colourize("CDF", "blue")` describes how `r colourize("probability", "blue")` accumulates up to a given value.

## D {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Data leakage", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Data leakage", "magenta")` occurs when information from outside the proper training process is allowed to influence **model fitting**, **model selection**, or **predictive assessment** in a way that would not be available when making predictions on genuinely new observations. In practice, leakage can lead to performance results that look artificially strong because the model has, directly or indirectly, already "*seen*" information from the data used for evaluation.
 
### `r colourize("Dependent variable", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("dependent variable", "blue")` is the measurement or quantity of main interest in a `r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")` problem. It is the variable whose behaviour we aim to explain, model, or predict using one or more `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`.

Depending on the application, the `r colourize("dependent variable", "blue")` may represent a continuous quantity, a count, a binary result, a proportion, or another type of measurement. The nature of the `r colourize("dependent variable", "blue")` plays a fundamental role in determining the appropriate regression model and `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` for the analysis.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Endogenous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")`, `r colourize("output", "magenta")` or `r colourize("target", "magenta")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Discrete random variable", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` with support $\mathcal{Y}$. If $\mathcal{Y}$ is a finite set or a countably infinite set of possible values, then $Y$ is called a `r colourize("discrete random variable", "blue")`. `r colourize("Discrete random variables", "blue")` commonly appear as:

- **binary variables**, whose support contains two possible values;
- **categorical variables**, whose support contains three or more categories, either nominal or ordinal; or
- **count variables**, whose support contains nonnegative integer values.

### `r colourize("Dispersion", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

## E {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Endogenous variable", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("endogenous variable", "magenta")` is the measurement or quantity of main interest in a `r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")` problem. It is the variable whose behaviour we aim to explain, model, or predict using one or more `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`.

Depending on the application, the `r colourize("endogenous variable", "magenta")` may represent a continuous quantity, a count, a binary result, a proportion, or another type of measurement. The nature of the `r colourize("endogenous variable", "magenta")` plays a fundamental role in determining the appropriate regression model and `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` for the analysis.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Dependent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")`, `r colourize("output", "magenta")`, `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` or `r colourize("target", "magenta")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Equidispersion", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Estimate", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("estimate", "blue")` is the numerical value obtained after applying an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` to the observed data.

For example, if $325$ out of $500$ surveyed children prefer chocolate ice cream, then the observed `r colourize("estimate", "blue")` of $\pi$ is

$$
\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{obs}}
=
\bar{d}
=
\frac{325}{500}
=
0.65.
$$

Here, $\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ is the observed estimate.

### `r colourize("Estimator", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` is a rule or function of a `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` used to estimate an unknown `r colourize("parameter", "blue")`. Because an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` is computed from `r colourize("random variables", "blue")`, it is itself a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`.

For example, if

$$
D_1,\ldots,D_{n_d}
\overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi),
$$

then the sample proportion

$$
\hat{\pi}
=
\bar{D}
=
\frac{1}{n_d}
\sum_{i=1}^{n_d} D_i
$$

is an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` of $\pi$.

### `r colourize("Expected value", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` with support $\mathcal{Y}$. The `r colourize("expected  value", "blue")` or `r colourize("mean", "blue")`, of $Y$ is a `r colourize("probability", "blue")`-weighted average of the possible values of the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`.

If $Y$ is `r colourize("discrete", "blue")` with `r colourize("probability mass function (PMF)", "blue")` $p_Y(y)$, then

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y)
=
\sum_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} y\,p_Y(y).
$$

If $Y$ is `r colourize("continuous", "blue")` with `r colourize("probability density function (PDF)", "blue")` $f_Y(y)$, then

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y)
=
\int_{\mathcal{Y}} y\,f_Y(y)\,dy.
$$

The `r colourize("expected  value", "blue")` is a `r colourize("population", "blue")`-level summary: it describes the centre of the `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")`, not the average of one particular observed sample.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Mean", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Exogeneous variable", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Exogeneous variables", "magenta")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("exogeneous variables", "magenta")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")`, `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Explanatory variable", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Explanatory variables", "blue")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")`, `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

## F {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("False negative", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("false negative", "blue")` occurs when a test **fails to reject** the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` $H_0$ even though $H_0$ is false in a scientifically or practically relevant way. This is also known as `r colourize("Type II error", "blue")`: the test does not detect a departure from the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`, even though such a departure is actually present.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Type II error", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("False positive", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("false positive", "blue")` occurs when a test **rejects** the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` $H_0$ even though $H_0$ is true. This is also known as `r colourize("Type I error", "blue")`: the test concludes that there is enough evidence against the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`, but the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` is actually true.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Type I error", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Feature", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("feature", "magenta")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Features", "magenta")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("features", "magenta")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")`, `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Fitted value", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

In a regression model, the `r colourize("fitted value", "blue")` for the $i$th observation is the model's `r colourize("estimate", "blue")` of the `r colourize("conditional mean", "blue")` of the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` given the $k$ observed `r colourize("regressors", "blue")`. After estimating the model `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` from the `r colourize("training data", "magenta")`, we plug the $i$th row of `r colourize("regressor", "blue")` values into the fitted systematic component to obtain

$$
\hat{y}_i = \widehat{\mathbb{E}}\left(Y_i \mid x_{i,1}, \ldots , x_{i,k}\right).
$$

### `r colourize("Frequentist statistics", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Frequentist statistics", "blue")` treats unknown `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` as fixed but unknown quantities. Randomness comes from the data-generating process, the sampling process, or the experimental assignment mechanism. Under this view, `r colourize("probabilities", "blue")` describe the long-run behaviour of random quantities under **repeated use of the same procedure**. For example, a `r colourize("standard error", "blue")` describes how much an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` would vary across repeated `r colourize("random samples", "blue")`, and a confidence level describes the long-run coverage behaviour of an interval procedure.

## G {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Generalized linear models", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

An umbrella of regression approaches that model the conditional `r colourize("expected value", "blue")` of the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` $Y$ based on a set of observed `r colourize("regressors", "blue")` $x$. Unlike a traditional model such as the `r colourize("continuous", "blue")` Ordinary Least-squares (OLS) that relies solely on a Normal distribution **to make inference**, `r colourize("GLMs", "blue")` extend this distributional assumption, allowing for a variety of `r colourize("probability distributions", "blue")` for the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`. Note that this umbrella encompasses approaches that accommodate `r colourize("continuous", "blue")` or `r colourize("discrete", "blue")` responses $Y$. According to @casella2024, a typical `r colourize("GLM", "blue")` consists of three key components:

- **Random Component:** The `r colourize("response variables", "blue")` in a `r colourize("training dataset", "magenta")` of size $n$ (i.e., the `r colourize("random variables", "blue")` $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$) are **statistically `r colourize("independent", "blue")` but not identically distributed**. Still, they do belong to the same family of `r colourize("probability distributions", "blue")` (e.g., Gamma, Beta, Poisson, Bernoulli, etc.).
- **Systematic Component:** For the $i$th observation, this component depicts how the $k$ `r colourize("regressors", "blue")` $x_{i, j}$ (for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$) come into the `r colourize("GLM", "blue")` as a linear combination involving $k + 1$ regression `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_k$. This relationship is expressed as

$$
\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i, 1} + \beta_2 x_{i, 2} + \ldots + \beta_k x_{i, k}.
$$

- **Link Function:** This component connects (or "*links*") the systematic component $\eta_i$ with the `r colourize("mean", "blue")` of the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` $Y_i$, denoted as $\mu_i$. The link function is mathematically represented as

$$
g(\mu_i) = \eta_i.
$$

@nelder1972 introduced **this umbrella term called `r colourize("GLM", "blue")`** in the statistical literature and identified a set of distinct statistical models that shared the above three components.

### `r colourize("Goodness of fit", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Goodness of fit", "blue")` refers to the process of assessing whether a fitted `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` is compatible with the main patterns in the observed data. A `r colourize("goodness-of-fit", "blue")` assessment does not prove that the model is true. Instead, it asks whether the fitted model is adequate enough for the inferential or predictive purpose of the `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")`.

## H {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Hypothesis testing", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Hypothesis testing", "blue")` is a `r colourize("frequentist", "blue")` decision procedure that uses sample data to assess evidence against a `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`. A `r colourize("hypothesis test", "blue")` compares the observed data with what would be expected under the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`. The outcome is not a statement that the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` is true or false with certainty. Instead, it is a conclusion about whether the observed evidence is strong enough, according to the chosen `r colourize("significance level", "blue")` and test procedure, to reject the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`.

## I {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Independence", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Two events $A$ and $B$ are `r colourize("independent", "blue")` if knowing that one event occurred does not change the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of the other event. Equivalently, if $\Pr(B) > 0$, then the @eq-conditional-probability-app for `r colourize("conditional probability", "blue")` simplifies to

$$
\Pr(A \mid B) = \Pr(A).
$$

Using the multiplication rule, this is equivalent to

$$
\Pr(A \cap B)
=
\Pr(A) \times \Pr(B).
$$

### `r colourize("Independence for random variables", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Random variables", "blue")` $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$ are **mutually `r colourize("independent", "blue")`** if their joint `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")` factors into the product of their marginal distributions.

The **joint `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")`**, often shortened to the **joint distribution**, describes the probabilistic behaviour of the full collection of `r colourize("random variables", "blue")` together. For example, it assigns `r colourize("probability", "blue")` or `r colourize("density", "blue")` to the vector of possible values

$$
\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)^{\top}.
$$

A **marginal `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")`**, often shortened to a **marginal distribution**, describes the probabilistic behaviour of one `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` on its own, without explicitly conditioning on or modelling the others. For example, $p_{Y_i}(y_i)$ is the marginal `r colourize("probability mass function (PMF)", "blue")` of the single random variable $Y_i$, while $f_{Y_i}(y_i)$ is the marginal `r colourize("probability density function (PDF)", "blue")` of the single `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` $Y_i$.

Thus, `r colourize("independence", "blue")` says that the joint behaviour of the full collection can be recovered by multiplying the separate one-variable behaviours.

If $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$ are `r colourize("discrete random variables", "blue")` with marginal `r colourize("PMFs", "blue")` $p_{Y_1}(y_1), p_{Y_2}(y_2), \ldots, p_{Y_n}(y_n)$, then `r colourize("independence", "blue")` implies

$$
p_{Y_1,\ldots,Y_n}(y_1,\ldots,y_n)
=
\prod_{i=1}^n p_{Y_i}(y_i).
$$

If $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$ are `r colourize("continuous random variables", "blue")`with marginal `r colourize("PDFs", "blue")` $f_{Y_1}(y_1), f_{Y_2}(y_2), \ldots, f_{Y_n}(y_n)$, then `r colourize("independence", "blue")` implies

$$
f_{Y_1,\ldots,Y_n}(y_1,\ldots,y_n)
=
\prod_{i=1}^n f_{Y_i}(y_i).
$$

### `r colourize("Independent variable", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Independent variables", "blue")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("independent variables", "blue")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")`, `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Input", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("input", "magenta")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Inputs", "magenta")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("inputs", "magenta")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

## L {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Likelihood function", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Suppose $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n$ are modelled as an i.i.d. `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` from a `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` with `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}
=
(\theta_1,\theta_2,\ldots,\theta_k)^\top
\in \Theta.
$$

After observing data

$$
\mathbf{y}
=
(y_1,\ldots,y_n)^\top,
$$

the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")` is the joint `r colourize("probability mass function (PMF)", "blue")` or joint `r colourize("probability density function (PDF)", "blue")` of the observed data, viewed as a function of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

For a `r colourize("discrete", "blue")` i.i.d. model with common `r colourize("PMF", "blue")` $p_Y(y;\boldsymbol{\theta})$, the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")` is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n p_Y(y_i;\boldsymbol{\theta}).
$$

For a `r colourize("continuous", "blue")` i.i.d. model with common `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` $f_Y(y;\boldsymbol{\theta})$, the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")` is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n f_Y(y_i;\boldsymbol{\theta}).
$$

The observed data $\mathbf{y}$ appear after the semicolon because they are fixed once collected. The `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is the input of the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")` because maximum likelihood estimation compares candidate `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` values.

### `r colourize("Log-likelihood function", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Suppose $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n$ are modelled as an i.i.d. `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` from a `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` with `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}
=
(\theta_1,\theta_2,\ldots,\theta_k)^\top
\in \Theta.
$$

After observing data

$$
\mathbf{y}
=
(y_1,\ldots,y_n)^\top,
$$

Then, the `r colourize("log-likelihood function", "blue")` is the natural logarithm of the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")`. We denote it by the lowercase script-like symbol $\ell(\cdot)$:

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})
=
\log\left[
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})
\right].
$$

Here, $\ell(\cdot)$ is not a new `r colourize("probability model", "blue")`. It is simply the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")` viewed on the logarithmic scale.

For a `r colourize("discrete", "blue")` i.i.d. model,

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
\log p_Y(y_i;\boldsymbol{\theta}),
$$

and for a `r colourize("continuous", "blue")` i.i.d. model,

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
\log f_Y(y_i;\boldsymbol{\theta}).
$$

## M {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Maximum likelihood estimation", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})$ be the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")` for observed data $\mathbf{y}$ under a `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` with `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta$. The `r colourize("maximum likelihood estimate", "blue")` is the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` value that maximizes the `r colourize("likelihood function", "blue")`:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}
=
\underset{\boldsymbol{\theta}\in\Theta}{\operatorname{arg\,max}}
\ 
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y}).
$$

Equivalently, because $\log(\cdot)$ is strictly increasing, we usually maximize the `r colourize("log-likelihood function", "blue")`:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}
=
\underset{\boldsymbol{\theta}\in\Theta}{\operatorname{arg\,max}}
\ 
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y}).
$$

In `r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")`, this idea is closely related to model training through loss minimization. Instead of saying that the model finds `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` values that maximize the `r colourize("log-likelihood", "blue")`,

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}
=
\underset{\boldsymbol{\theta}\in\Theta}{\operatorname{arg\,max}}
\ 
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y}),
$$

we can equivalently say that it finds parameter values that minimize the **negative `r colourize("log-likelihood", "blue")`**:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}
=
\underset{\boldsymbol{\theta}\in\Theta}{\operatorname{arg\,min}}
\ 
\left[-\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})\right].
$$

Thus, the **negative `r colourize("log-likelihood", "blue")`** can be interpreted as a loss function: smaller values indicate that the fitted model is more compatible with the observed data under the chosen `r colourize("probability model", "blue")`. This connection helps explain why `r colourize("maximum likelihood estimation", "blue")` appears so often behind the scenes in regression and machine learning software.

### `r colourize("Mean", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` with support $\mathcal{Y}$. The `r colourize("mean", "blue")` or `r colourize("expected value", "blue")`, of $Y$ is a `r colourize("probability", "blue")`-weighted average of the possible values of the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`.

If $Y$ is `r colourize("discrete", "blue")` with `r colourize("probability mass function (PMF)", "blue")` $p_Y(y)$, then

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y)
=
\sum_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} y\,p_Y(y).
$$

If $Y$ is `r colourize("continuous", "blue")` with `r colourize("probability density function (PDF)", "blue")` $f_Y(y)$, then

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y)
=
\int_{\mathcal{Y}} y\,f_Y(y)\,dy.
$$

The `r colourize("mean", "blue")` is a `r colourize("population", "blue")`-level summary: it describes the centre of the `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")`, not the average of one particular observed sample.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Expected value", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Measure of central tendency", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("measure of central tendency", "blue")`  is a summary that identifies a central or typical value of a `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")`. Probabilistically, it describes where a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` tends to be located when we imagine observing many realizations from the same `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system", "blue")`.

### `r colourize("Measure of uncertainty", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("measure of uncertainty", "blue")` summarizes the spread or variability of a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` around its `r colourize("central tendency", "blue")`. Larger values indicate that the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` tends to vary more across realizations, while smaller values indicate that realizations tend to be more tightly concentrated.

## N {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Null hypothesis", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`, denoted by $H_0$, is the reference statement about a `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system parameter", "blue")`. It often represents a status quo, benchmark value, absence of effect, or simpler model. Mathematically, if the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector is $\boldsymbol{\theta}\in\Theta$, the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` can be written as

$$
H_0 \text{: }
\boldsymbol{\theta}
\in
\Theta_0,
\qquad
\Theta_0 \subseteq \Theta.
$$

In practice, the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` should be translated into plain language so that the statistical test remains connected to the original inquiry.

### `r colourize("Null distribution", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("null distribution", "blue")` is the `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")` of a `r colourize("test statistic", "blue")` computed under the assumption that the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` is true. It tells us what values of the `r colourize("test statistic", "blue")` would be ordinary or unusual if the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` were the correct reference condition.

## O {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Observed effect", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("observed effect", "blue")` is the difference between an observed `r colourize("estimate", "blue")` and the value of the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` specified by the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`. For a scalar `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` $\theta$, an `r colourize("observed effect", "blue")` often has the form

$$
\hat{\theta}_{\operatorname{obs}} - \theta_0,
$$

where $\hat{\theta}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ is the `r colourize("estimate", "blue")` computed from the observed sample and $\theta_0$ is the null value.

### `r colourize("Outcome", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")` is the measurement or quantity of main interest in a `r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")` problem. It is the variable whose behaviour we aim to explain, model, or predict using one or more `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`.

Depending on the application, the `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")` may represent a continuous quantity, a count, a binary result, a proportion, or another type of measurement. The nature of the `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")` plays a fundamental role in determining the appropriate regression model and `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` for the analysis.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Dependent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("endogenous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("output", "magenta")` or `r colourize("target", "magenta")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Output", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

An `r colourize("output", "magenta")` is the measurement or quantity of main interest in a `r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")` problem. It is the variable whose behaviour we aim to explain, model, or predict using one or more `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`.

Depending on the application, the `r colourize("output", "magenta")` may represent a continuous quantity, a count, a binary result, a proportion, or another type of measurement. The nature of the `r colourize("output", "magenta")` plays a fundamental role in determining the appropriate regression model and `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` for the analysis.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Dependent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("endogenous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")` or `r colourize("target", "magenta")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Overdispersion", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

## P {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Parameter", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` is an unknown characteristic that summarizes some feature of a `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system", "blue")`. `r colourize("Parameters", "blue")` are commonly numerical, such as a `r colourize("mean", "blue")`, `r colourize("variance", "blue")`, `r colourize("probability", "blue")`, rate, or a regression coefficient, although some summaries can be categorical in non-regression settings.

In this book, scalar `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` are usually be denoted by Greek letters such as $\theta$, $\mu$, $\sigma^2$, $\lambda$, or $\beta_j$ (for more insights, see @sec-greek-alphabet). The word **scalar** means that the `r colourize("population parameter", "blue")` is a single numerical quantity. For example, $\mu$ may denote one `r colourize("population mean", "blue")`, $\lambda$ may denote one rate `r colourize("parameter", "blue")`, and $\beta_j$ may denote one regression coefficient associated with the $j$th `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.

When a model contains several `r colourize("parameters", "blue")`, it is often more convenient to collect them into a vector. A **`r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector** will usually be denoted by a bold symbol such as $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ or $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. For instance, in a regression model with an intercept and $k$ `r colourize("regressors", "blue")`, we may write

$$
\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_k)^\top.
$$

Here, $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ denotes the full vector of regression coefficients, while each $\beta_j$ ($j = 1, \dots, k$) denotes one scalar component of that vector. This notation helps us distinguish between questions about a single coefficient, such as whether $\beta_1 = 0$, and questions about the collection of `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` that defines the fitted regression model as a whole.

### `r colourize("Parametric family", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("parametric family", "blue")` is a collection of `r colourize("probability distributions", "blue")` that share the same mathematical form but differ according to the value of one or more unknown `r colourize("parameters", "blue")`. If $Y$ is a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` and $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ denotes a `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector, we can write a `r colourize("parametric family", "blue")` abstractly as

$$
\{p_Y(y; \boldsymbol{\theta}) : \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta\}
$$

for a `r colourize("discrete random variable", "blue")`, or as

$$
\{f_Y(y; \boldsymbol{\theta}) : \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta\}
$$

for a `r colourize("continuous random variable", "blue")`.

Here, $\Theta$ denotes the **`r colourize("parameter", "blue")` space**, which is the set of possible values the `r colourize("parameter space", "blue")` vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ can take. Each specific value of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ identifies one member of the family.

### `r colourize("Parametric model", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("parametric model", "blue")` is a type of model that assumes a specific functional relationship between the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` of interest, $Y$, which is considered a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`, and one or more observed `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`, $x$. This relationship is characterized by a finite set of `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` and can often be expressed as a linear combination of the observed $x$ variables, which favours **interpretability**.

Moreover, since $Y$ is a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`, there is room to make further assumptions on it in the form of a `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")`, `r colourize("independence", "blue")` or even homoscedasticity (the condition where all responses in the `r colourize("population", "blue")` have the same `r colourize("variance", "blue")`). It is essential to test these assumptions after fitting this type of models, as any deviations may result in **misleading or biased** `r colourize("estimates", "blue")`, predictions, and inferential conclusions.

### `r colourize("Point estimate", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("point estimate", "blue")` is a single numerical value used to estimate an unknown `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system parameter", "blue")`. It is obtained by applying an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` to the observed data.

### `r colourize("Population", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("population", "blue")` is the whole collection of individuals or items that share the attributes we want to study. In a statistical analysis, the `r colourize("population", "blue")` should be defined as precisely as possible, as it determines the scope of the conclusions we can draw.

### `r colourize("Power", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("power", "blue")` of a `r colourize("hypothesis test", "blue")` is the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of rejecting the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` ($H_0$) when a particular `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")` ($H_1$) is true:

$$
\operatorname{Power}
=
\Pr(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ is true in a specified way}).
$$

If $\beta$ denotes the  `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of a  `r colourize("Type II error", "blue")` for a specified `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")`, then

$$
\operatorname{Power}
=
1-\beta.
$$

`r colourize("Power", "blue")` depends on the sample size, `r colourize("significance level", "blue")`, variability, test direction, and effect size that the study aims to detect.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("True positive rate", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Power analysis", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("power analysis", "blue")` is a set of statistical tools used to compute the **minimum required sample size $n$** for any given inferential study. These tools require the `r colourize("significance level", "blue")`, `r colourize("power", "blue")`, and **effect size** (i.e., the **magnitude of the signal**) the researcher aims to detect via their inferential study. This analysis seeks to determine whether observed results are likely **due to chance** or represent a **true and meaningful effect**.

### `r colourize("Predictor", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Predictors", "magenta")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("predictors", "magenta")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regressor", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Probability", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $A$ be an event in a random phenomenon with `r colourize("sample space", "blue")` $\mathcal{S}$. The `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of event $A$ is denoted by

$$
\Pr(A).
$$

Under a `r colourize("frequentist", "blue")` interpretation, $\Pr(A)$ can be understood as the limiting relative frequency of event $A$ over repeated observations of the same random phenomenon:

$$
\Pr(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\text{Number of times event } A \text{ is observed in } n \text{ repetitions}}{n}.
$$

Note that a probability must satisfy

$$
0 \leq \Pr(A) \leq 1.
$$

### `r colourize("Probability distribution", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` with support $\mathcal{Y}$. A `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")` describes how `r colourize("probability", "blue")` is assigned to the possible values of $Y$:

- If $Y$ is `r colourize("discrete", "blue")`, the distribution assigns `r colourize("probability", "blue")` to individual values in $\mathcal{Y}$. 
- If $Y$ is `r colourize("continuous", "blue")`, the distribution assigns `r colourize("probability", "blue")` to intervals of values in $\mathcal{Y}$.

### `r colourize("Probability density function", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("continuous random variable", "blue")` with support $\mathcal{Y}$. A `r colourize("probability density function (PDF)", "blue")` is a function $f_Y(y)$ used to compute `r colourize("probabilities", "blue")` over intervals. Specifically, for two values $a$ and $b$ in the support of $Y$, with $a \leq b$,

$$
\Pr(a \leq Y \leq b)
=
\int_a^b f_Y(y)\,dy.
$$

Thus, for a `r colourize("continuous random variable", "blue")`, the `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` is not itself a `r colourize("probability", "blue")` at a single point. Instead, `r colourize("probabilities", "blue")` are obtained by integrating the `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` over intervals.

A valid `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` must satisfy two conditions:

$$
f_Y(y) \geq 0
\quad \text{for all } y \in \mathcal{Y},
$$

and

$$
\int_{\mathcal{Y}} f_Y(y)\,dy = 1.
$$

If the `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` depends on an unknown `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` or `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector, we will write this dependence using a semicolon. For example,

$$
f_Y(y;\boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

denotes the `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` of $Y$ evaluated at $y$, under a `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` controlled by the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. The value before the semicolon, $y$, is a possible value of the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`. The quantity after the semicolon, $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, contains the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` or `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` that determine the shape or behaviour of the density.

### `r colourize("Probability mass function", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("discrete random variable", "blue")` with support $\mathcal{Y}$. The `r colourize("probability mass function (PMF)", "blue")` of $Y$ is the function

$$
p_Y(y) = \Pr(Y = y),
$$

which assigns a `r colourize("probability", "blue")` to each possible value $y \in \mathcal{Y}$. Thus, for any possible value $y$, the `r colourize("PMF", "blue")` evaluated at $y$ gives the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` that the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` $Y$ takes that value. A valid `r colourize("PMF", "blue")` must satisfy

$$
p_Y(y) \geq 0
\quad \text{for all } y \in \mathcal{Y},
$$

and

$$
\sum_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} p_Y(y) = 1.
$$

For values outside the support, we define

$$
p_Y(y) = 0
\quad \text{for } y \notin \mathcal{Y}.
$$

If the `r colourize("PMF", "blue")` depends on an unknown `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` or `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector, we will write this dependence using a semicolon. For example,

$$
p_Y(y;\boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

denotes the `r colourize("PMF", "blue")` of $Y$ evaluated at $y$, under a `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` controlled by the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. The value before the semicolon, $y$, is a possible value of the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`. The quantity after the semicolon, $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, contains the `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` or `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` that determine the `r colourize("probabilities", "blue")` assigned by the model.

### `r colourize("Probability model", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` is a mathematical representation of how a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` behaves under uncertainty in a `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system", "blue")` of interest. It specifies the possible values the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` can take together with a distribution that describes how probable those values are, usually through one or more governing `r colourize("parameters", "blue")`. This idea aligns with the notion of a **generative model**, where we use a `r colourize("probabiity distribution", "blue")` to describe how the observed data could have been generated from the `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system", "blue")` under study.

### `r colourize("$p$-value", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("$p$-value", "blue")` is the `r colourize("probability", "blue")`, computed under the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`, of observing a `r colourize("test statistic", "blue")` as extreme as or more extreme than the one obtained from the observed sample, in the direction specified by the `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")`. The $p$-value is not the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` that the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` is true. It is a `r colourize("probability", "blue")` about the `r colourize("test statistic", "blue")` under the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`.

## R {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Random sample", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` is a collection of `r colourize("random variables", "blue")`

$$
Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n
$$

with support $\mathcal{Y}$, that are commonly assumed to be **`r colourize("independent", "blue")` and identically distributed** from the same `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")`. This assumption is abbreviated as **i.i.d.**

The phrase **identically distributed** means that each `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` in the sample is governed by the same `r colourize("probability model", "blue")`. The phrase **`r colourize("independent", "blue")`** means that the joint `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")` of the full sample factors into the product of the marginal distributions of the individual observations.

If the common distribution is represented by $F_Y$, we write

$$
Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n
\overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim}
F_Y.
$$

More explicitly, if the common `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` depends on an unknown `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, then each observation is governed by the same `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector. The notation $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ may contain one `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` or several `r colourize("parameters", "blue")`, depending on the model. For example, $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ could contain only one `r colourize("probability parameter", "blue")`, such as $\pi$, or it could contain several regression coefficients, such as

$$
\boldsymbol{\beta}
=
(\beta_0,\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_k)^\top.
$$

For a `r colourize("discrete", "blue")` i.i.d. `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` with common `r colourize("probability mass function (PMF)", "blue")` $p_Y(y;\boldsymbol{\theta})$, the joint `r colourize("PMF", "blue")` is

$$
p_{Y_1,\ldots,Y_n}(y_1,\ldots,y_n;\boldsymbol{\theta})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n p_Y(y_i;\boldsymbol{\theta}),
\qquad
y_i \in \mathcal{Y}.
$${#eq-joint-pmf-iid-random-variables-app}

For a `r colourize("continuous", "blue")` i.i.d. `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` with common `r colourize("probability density function (PDF)", "blue")` $f_Y(y;\boldsymbol{\theta})$, the joint `r colourize("PDF", "blue")` is

$$
f_{Y_1,\ldots,Y_n}(y_1,\ldots,y_n;\boldsymbol{\theta})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n f_Y(y_i;\boldsymbol{\theta}),
\qquad
y_i \in \mathcal{Y}.
$${#eq-joint-pdf-iid-random-variables-app}

The semicolon in @eq-joint-pmf-iid-random-variables-app and @eq-joint-pdf-iid-random-variables-app follows the notation convention for parametrized `r colourize("PMFs", "blue")` and `r colourize("PDFs", "blue")`. The values $y_1,\ldots,y_n$ appear before the semicolon because they are possible observed values of the `r colourize("random variables", "blue")`. The `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ appears after the semicolon because it is part of the model specification.

Once data are collected, the realized values are written as

$$
y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n.
$$

The uppercase symbols $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n$ denote the `r colourize("random variables", "blue")` before observation, while the lowercase symbols $y_1,\ldots,y_n$ denote the observed values after data collection.

### `r colourize("Random variable", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` is a function that assigns a numerical value to each possible outcome of a random phenomenon. Before the phenomenon is observed, the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` represents uncertainty. After observation, we obtain a realized value. In this book, an uppercase letter such as $Y$ denotes a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`, whereas a lowercase value such as $y_i$ denotes the $i$th observed realization.

### `r colourize("Regression analysis", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Regression analysis", "blue")` is a collection of **statistical methods** used to study the relationship between a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` and one or more `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`. Its goals may include explaining associations, estimating effects, quantifying uncertainty, or predicting future `r colourize("outcomes", "magenta")`.

More broadly, `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")` provides a principled framework for connecting a scientific or practical question to data, a `r colourize("probability model", "blue")`, an estimation method, and an interpretation of results. The specific form of the regression model depends on the nature of the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`, the modelling assumptions, and whether the main objective is inference, prediction, or both.

### `r colourize("Regressor", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("regressor", "blue")` is a variable used to help explain, describe, or predict the behaviour of a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` in a regression model. `r colourize("Regressors", "blue")` represent the observed characteristics or conditions associated with each observational unit. Depending on the context, `r colourize("regressors", "blue")` may be quantitative or categorical, and their role may be primarily explanatory, predictive, or both.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Attribute", "magenta")`, `r colourize("covariate", "blue")`, `r colourize("exogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("explanatory variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("feature", "magenta")`, `r colourize("independent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("input", "magenta")` or `r colourize("predictor", "magenta")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Residual", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("residual", "blue")` is the difference between the observed `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` and its `r colourize("fitted value", "blue")` from the model. For the $i$th observation, the `r colourize("residual", "blue")` is

$$
e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i,
$$

where $y_i$ is the observed `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` and $\hat{y}_i$ is the `r colourize("fitted value", "blue")`.

`r colourize("Residuals", "blue")` measure the part of the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` that the fitted model did not explain through its systematic component. Many goodness-of-fit diagnostics are built by inspecting whether these `r colourize("residuals", "blue")` behave like the model's assumed "*random noise*."

### `r colourize("Response variable", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` is the measurement or quantity of main interest in a `r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")` problem. It is the variable whose behaviour we aim to explain, model, or predict using one or more `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`.

Depending on the application, the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` may represent a continuous quantity, a count, a binary result, a proportion, or another type of measurement. The nature of the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")` plays a fundamental role in determining the appropriate regression model and `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` for the analysis.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Dependent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("endogeneous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")`, `r colourize("output", "magenta")` or `r colourize("target", "magenta")`.
::::
:::

## S {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Sample space", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("sample space", "blue")` $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of all possible outcomes of a random phenomenon. An **event** $A$ is a subset of the `r colourize("sample space", "blue")`, so

$$
A \subseteq \mathcal{S}.
$$ 

Note that the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of the whole `r colourize("sample space", "blue")` is

$$
\Pr(\mathcal{S}) = 1.
$$

### `r colourize("Sampling distribution", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("sampling distribution", "blue")` of an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` is the `r colourize("probability distribution", "blue")` of that `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` across repeated `r colourize("random samples", "blue")` from the same `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system", "blue")` under the same sampling design.

### `r colourize("Sampling variability", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Sampling variability", "blue")` is the variation in an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` across repeated `r colourize("random samples", "blue")` from the same `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system", "blue")` under the same sampling design. It reflects the fact that an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` is a `r colourize("random variable", "blue")` before the data are observed.

### `r colourize("Semiparametric model", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A semiparametric model is a statistical model that incorporates **both parametric and nonparametric parts**. In the context of linear regression, these parts can be described as follows:

- The **parametric part** includes the systematic component where $k$ observed `r colourize("regressors", "blue")` $x$ are modelled along with $k + 1$ regression `r colourize("parameters", "blue")` $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_k$ in a linear combination.
- The **nonparametric part** does not impose specific assumptions on one or more modelling components, allowing the observed `r colourize("training dataset", "magenta")` to estimate these elements without requiring any `r colourize("probability distributions", "blue")`.

### `r colourize("Significance level", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("significance level", "blue")`, denoted by $\alpha$, is the long-run `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of rejecting the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` ($H_0$) when the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` is true:

$$
\alpha
=
\Pr(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true}).
$$

In plain language, $\alpha$ is the test procedure's tolerated `r colourize("Type I error rate", "blue")`. A smaller $\alpha$ makes it harder to reject $H_0$ and reduces the long-run `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of a `r colourize("Type I error rate", "blue")`, at the cost of making the test less sensitive to some alternatives.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Type I error rate", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Standard error in estimation", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("standard error", "blue")` of an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` is the standard deviation of its `r colourize("sampling distribution", "blue")`. It quantifies how much the `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` would vary across repeated `r colourize("random samples", "blue")`. In general, for an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")` $\hat{\theta}$,

$$
\operatorname{SE}(\hat{\theta})
=
\operatorname{SD}(\hat{\theta}).
$$

In practice, the `r colourize("standard error", "blue")` is usually estimated from the observed data.

### `r colourize("Standard error in inference", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("standard error", "blue")` measures the repeated-sampling variability of an `r colourize("estimator", "blue")`. In `r colourize("hypothesis testing", "blue")`, the `r colourize("standard error", "blue")` is used to judge whether the `r colourize("observed effect", "blue")` is large or small relative to the variability expected under the sampling process.

### `r colourize("Statistical hypothesis", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("statistical hypothesis", "blue")` is a statement about an unknown `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system parameter", "blue")`. Suppose a `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` contains a `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` vector

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}
=
(\theta_1,\theta_2,\ldots,\theta_k)^{\top},
$$

with `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` space $\Theta$. A `r colourize("statistical hypothesis", "blue")` restricts the possible values of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ to a subset of that `r colourize("parameter", "blue")` space:

$$
H \text{: }
\boldsymbol{\theta}
\in
\Theta^*,
\qquad
\Theta^* \subseteq \Theta.
$$

In applied work, this mathematical statement should also have a plain-language interpretation connected to the data science inquiry.

### `r colourize("Supervised learning", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

`r colourize("Supervised learning", "magenta")` is a data modelling framework in which we use observed pairs $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ for the $i$th observation, where $\mathbf{x}_i$ denotes the `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")` recorded for that observation, to learn a rule or function that maps `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")` to a `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`. The goal may be primarily **predictive** (accurate future predictions), primarily **inferential** (understanding associations or effects between $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $y_i$), or a mixture of both.

### `r colourize("Survival analysis", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("System", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("system", "blue")` is a process, mechanism, or operational setting whose behaviour is governed by unknown features we want to study. The word `r colourize("system", "blue")` is useful when the object of study is not naturally a collection of people or items.

## T {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Target", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("target", "magenta")` is the measurement or quantity of main interest in a `r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")` or `r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")` problem. It is the variable whose behaviour we aim to explain, model, or predict using one or more `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`.

Depending on the application, the `r colourize("target", "magenta")` may represent a continuous quantity, a count, a binary result, a proportion, or another type of measurement. The nature of the `r colourize("target", "magenta")` plays a fundamental role in determining the appropriate regression model and `r colourize("probability model", "blue")` for the analysis.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Dependent variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("endogenous variable", "magenta")`, `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`, `r colourize("outcome", "magenta")` or `r colourize("output", "magenta")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Test set", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("test set", "magenta")` (or **testing set**) is the subset of the **observed `r colourize("random sample", "blue")`** reserved for a final and unbiased assessment of model performance after all model-building decisions have been completed. Its role is to provide a trustworthy evaluation of how well the chosen model is expected to perform on future or unseen observations from the same `r colourize("population", "blue")` or `r colourize("system", "blue")` of interest in **predictive settings**.

On the other hand, in **inferential settings**, the `r colourize("test set", "magenta")` may serve a protective role against **double dipping**, since it allows us to reserve part of the observed `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` for a more formal assessment (via `r colourize("hypothesis testing", "blue")`) after exploratory work has been carried out on the `r colourize("training set", "magenta")`.

### `r colourize("Test statistic", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("test statistic", "blue")`  is a numerical summary of the observed sample used to measure how far the observed evidence is from the reference behaviour implied by the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`.

### `r colourize("Training set", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("training set", "magenta")` is the subset of the **observed `r colourize("random sample", "blue")`** used to fit or estimate one or more candidate models. In **`r colourize("regression analysis", "blue")`**, this means that the `r colourize("training set", "magenta")` is used to estimate unknown model terms, such as regression coefficients, and to carry out classical diagnostic checks on the fitted model.

In a **`r colourize("supervised learning", "magenta")` context**, the `r colourize("training set", "magenta")` is the portion of the observed `r colourize("random sample", "blue")` from which the model primarily learns the relationship between the `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")` and the `r colourize("response variable", "blue")`.

### `r colourize("True positive rate", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("true positive rate", "blue")` (also known as `r colourize("power", "blue")`) of a `r colourize("hypothesis test", "blue")` is the `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of rejecting the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` $(H_0)$ when a particular `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")` ($H_1$) is true:

$$
\operatorname{Power}
=
\Pr(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ is true in a specified way}).
$$

If $\beta$ denotes the  `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of a  `r colourize("Type II error", "blue")` for a specified `r colourize("alternative hypothesis", "blue")`, then

$$
\operatorname{Power}
=
1-\beta.
$$

The `r colourize("true positive rate", "blue")` depends on the sample size, `r colourize("significance level", "blue")`, variability, test direction, and effect size that the study aims to detect.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Power", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Type I error rate", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

The `r colourize("Type I error rate ", "blue")`, denoted by $\alpha$, is the long-run `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of rejecting the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` ($H_0$) when the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` is true:

$$
\alpha
=
\Pr(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true}).
$$

In other words, $\alpha$ is the test procedure's tolerated `r colourize("significance level", "blue")`. A smaller $\alpha$ makes it harder to reject $H_0$ and reduces the long-run `r colourize("probability", "blue")` of a `r colourize("Type I error rate", "blue")`, at the cost of making the test less sensitive to some alternatives.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("Significance level", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Type I error", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("Type I error", "blue")` occurs when a test **rejects** the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` $H_0$ even though $H_0$ is true. In plain language, this is a `r colourize("false positive", "blue")`: the test concludes that there is enough evidence against the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`, but the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` is actually true.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("False positive", "blue")`.
::::
:::

### `r colourize("Type II error", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("Type II error", "blue")` occurs when a test **fails to reject** the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")` $H_0$ even though $H_0$ is false in a scientifically or practically relevant way. In plain language, this is a `r colourize("false negative", "blue")`: the test does not detect a departure from the `r colourize("null hypothesis", "blue")`, even though such a departure is actually present.

::: {.Equivalence}
::::{.Equivalence-header}
Equivalent to:
::::
::::{.Equivalence-container}
`r colourize("False negative", "blue")`.
::::
:::

## U {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Underdispersion", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

## V {.unnumbered}

### `r colourize("Validation set", "magenta")` {.unnumbered}

A `r colourize("validation set", "magenta")` is the subset of the **observed `r colourize("random sample", "blue")`** used to compare candidate models, modelling strategies, or tuning decisions after those models have been fitted on the `r colourize("training set", "magenta")`. Its main role is to assess how well different modelling choices generalize to unseen observations before a final model is selected.

In predictive regression, the `r colourize("validation set", "magenta")` is especially useful when comparing **alternative model specifications**, such as different sets of `r colourize("explanatory variables", "blue")`, transformations, or interaction terms. It is not primarily used for classical `r colourize("residual", "blue")` diagnostics, but rather for **model comparison** and **predictive assessment**.

### `r colourize("Variance", "blue")` {.unnumbered}

Let $Y$ be a `r colourize("discrete", "blue")` or `r colourize("continuous random variable", "blue")` with finite `r colourize("expected value", "blue")` $\mathbb{E}(Y)$. The `r colourize("variance", "blue")` of $Y$ is the expected squared deviation from its `r colourize("mean", "blue")`:

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y)
=
\mathbb{E}\left\{[Y - \mathbb{E}(Y)]^2\right\}.
$$

An equivalent expression is

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y)
=
\mathbb{E}(Y^2) - [\mathbb{E}(Y)]^2.
$$

The **standard deviation** of $Y$ is

$$
\operatorname{SD}(Y)
=
\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(Y)}.
$$

The standard deviation is often easier to interpret than the `r colourize("variance", "blue")` because it is measured in the same units as the `r colourize("random variable", "blue")`.
